19.1.label: Question 19.1
19.1.scenario:
‘Health and care insurance operates in a highly competitive market so surely market forces can be
relied on to keep prices at reasonable levels.’
19.1.question: Comment.

 A competitive market can lead:           regulator‐imposed prices could lead to insurers making larger profits for the
shareholders and/or could lead to insurers operating with poor efficiency, thereby
          complying with regulation incurs costs, so cost savings may be passed to
          Market‐driven prices allow insurers to react much more swiftly to relevant changes in
experience, particularly claims experience. If claims experience:
 improves, then prices can fal:           worsens, then insurers are able to take prompt action to ensure losses are not
made, which could have led to them exiting the market (thus reducing the level of
competition, which could have made prices higher).
Disadvantages of leaving it to market fo:           Collusion of insurers might result in contracts being sold at artificially high prices.
          Market forces may drive prices down, however, this might result in either a poor standard
of insurance service (in order to keep costs down), or the threat of insolvency of the insurer.
 A lack of regulation could le:           the over‐pricing of some contracts, eg customers with minority needs may end up
having to pay more (while popular contracts are competitively priced)
          some contracts not being provided at all, eg if they are deemed unprofitable
          Changes in market structure (eg companies dropping out, mergers and acquisitions) can
quickly change the competitive effectiveness of a market.

(i).question:
State what is meant by the ‘net present value’ (NPV) of a contract, and how it would be
              calculated.

Net present value (NPV): The NPV requires the calculation of the profit signature for a given model point.                       [½]
The profit signature is the expected (usually annual) future profit stream that would be generated
The first step is the projection of future cashflows, including both income (premiums or charges,
… and outgo (expenses, claim costs and tax).                                                            [½]
The outgo will also include the expected cost of increasing the supervisory reserve each year. [½]
The future profit flows are then adjusted to allow for future survival and withdrawal, to produce
The NPV is the discounted value of the profit signature at the risk discount rate.                 [½]

(ii).question: Explain the ways in which health insurers can make use of NPV calculations.

Using net present values: The NPV is an example of a profit criterion: a single figure that tries to summarise the relative
efficiency of contracts with different profit signatures.                                         [½]
By applying a profit criterion to different contracts with different profit signatures, and by ranking
the results in order, it may be possible to say with confidence which contract makes most efficient
Such a criterion can also be used to price a long‐term health and care insurance contract, by
finding the premium (or charges) that satisfies the required profit criterion, based on a given set
Given a choice between the future cashflows from two different investments, economic theory
states that an investor should choose the one with the higher NPV. This choice is optimal, and
Another way to put this is that the first priority for the managers of any company is to maximise
the net present worth of the company.                                                           [½]
Also, the NPV can be usefully expressed:          a percentage of the commission payable under the contract                                         [½]
         a percentage of the expected present value of the premiums payable under the contract.
Initial commission: then c shows the net present value relative to the effort expended selling the policy.                       [1]
Assuming that sales effort is geared towards maximising the commission paid to intermediaries,
then pricing using this criterion will ensure that the sales process will also maximise the
company’s profits, regardless of which products are actually sold.                           [1]
Expected present value of future premium: then the company will be sure that its profitability will increase in proportion to any increase in
market share that it can achieve for any product.                                                   [½]
The company would choose which of these to adopt on the basis of which factor was most
influential in driving sales: increasing commission payments or increasing market share.                    [½]
The use of NPVs is not confined to measuring the worth of new health and care insurance policies.
An insurer has other possible uses for its capital, such as the improvement of its administration
systems for the support of existing business, or the development of a new sales channel.          [½]
The company can use NPV to compare all kinds of capital investment projects, regardless of their

(iii).question:
Describe why a health insurer might choose to use the NPV as a profit criterion, rather
than the internal rate of return or the discounted payback period.

Why net present value might be chosen in: In part (ii) it was stated that, according to economic theory, the NPV cannot be bettered, so if any
other criterion disagrees with it then the company should go with the NPV.                        [½]
Internal rate of return (IRR): The IRR the rate of return at which the discounted value of the cashflows is zero.                      [½]
All other things being equal, a company should prefer a contract that has a higher IRR. However,
the IRR does not always agree with the NPV.                                                   [½]
Calculating an IRR requires an iterative approach, whereas a NPV does not, and so the latter is
NPV may be more reliable in some cases,:           If there is more than one change of sign in the stream of profits in the profit signature, the
IRR will not usually be unique.                                                            [½]
          The NPV can be related to useful indicators of the policy’s worth to the company, in terms
of sales effort or market share.                                                        [½]
There is no way to do this with the IRR.                                                     [½]
          If a policy makes profits from the outset then the IRR may not even exist. The NPV always
The NPV can allow for the risks of the project by adjusting the risk discount rate and/or by
including specific margins in the individual assumptions.                                               [½]
Only the second of these methods is possible using the IRR.                                             [½]
Discounted payback period (DPP): The DPP is the policy duration at which the profits which have emerged so far have present value
... ie it is the time it takes for the company to recover its initial investment with interest at the risk
A company with limited capital might prefer to sell contracts with as short a payback period as
The DPP will not usually agree with the NPV as it ignores cashflows after the DPP.                      [½]
Only the NPV provides any information about the size of the profit flow. Hence neither of the
other two methods can be used without considering the NPV as well.                            [½]

(iv).question: Describe the drawbacks of using the NPV as a profit criterion.

Comparisons based on the NPV will only b:           there is a perfectly free and efficient capital market, which is never completely true       [½]
          the risk discount rates used to discount two (or more) risky investments appropriately
reflect their riskiness, which may be very difficult to assess.                        [½]
The NPV is subject to the law of diminishing returns.                                                   [½]
If it were not, then a company that could sell one policy with positive NPV, could sell an unlimited
number of policies and increase the value of the firm without limit.                              [½]
So even a marketable and competitive product would run out of customers once the market is
saturated, and/or the cost of making more sales becomes increasingly prohibitive.          [½]
NPV says nothing about competition. There is no point in designing a contract with a high NPV if
However, these last two (related) problems can be effectively dealt with by comparing total NPVs
for whole projects, based on realistic assumptions of future new business sales.              [½]
If the expected cost of overheads is included in the NPV, this will also give a distorted comparison
if the assumed volume upon which the fixed expenses are based is incorrect.                       [½]
This can be avoided by including only marginal profit in the NPV calculation.                               [½]
The NPV does not distinguish between policies that have very different initial capital
requirements, and hence may have very different DPPs.                                                 [½]

19.4.label: Question 19.4
19.4.question: Describe the ways in which reinsurance can affect product pricing.

Solution: Reinsurance will usually have a net cost, reducing the expected profitability of the contract.              [½]
The product price may therefore need to be increased to reflect this cost.                                  [½]
Due to arbitrage possibilities (eg tax), it may be that reinsurance can lead to increased profit for
the insurer.                                                                                       [½]
This could result in a reduction in product price.                                                          [½]
Reinsurance generally reduces risk.                                                                         [½]
This means that the product can be priced with more certainty and so the margins in the basis to
cover risk (eg in the risk discount rate) do not need to be so large.                          [½]
This will reduce the product price.                                                                         [½]
Reinsurance can help reduce the insurer’s financing requirement.                                            [½]
This will reduce the cost of capital, and should also reduce the product price.                             [½]
It may also allow more business to be written.                                                              [½]
Reducing the price may also increase sales, so resulting in higher total profits.                           [½]
The reinsurance contract may specify the price that the insurer has to charge for a product, ... [½]
... eg when a quota share arrangement with a low retention level is used.                                   [½]
Any profit‐sharing agreements may affect the expected profit and hence the price that can be
charged.                                                                                     [½]
[Maximum 5]
(i)       Main reason health and care guarantees are not financially onerous
Most health and care insurance products are protection rather than savings contracts, in which
the main risk to the insurer arises from the incidence and (in some cases) duration of claims.
While the claim payment may be both large and guaranteed, the policy reserves are usually so
small relative to the size of the benefit that even if investment returns (on those reserves) are
much lower than expected, the financial impact on the insurer will be small.
(ii)      Contract that could pose significant investment risk
Pre‐funded long‐term care insurance is the one health and care contract that does usually require
large reserves, and for which a fall in investment returns could pose a significant risk. Whether or
not it does pose a risk will then depend on the extent to which the policy is written on guaranteed
terms. If all benefits and premiums / charges are fully guaranteed, then the investment risk could
be large.
Section 3 in this chapter focuses on issues relating to pricing business that offers guarantees.
As indicated earlier, the offering of guarantees needs to be considered carefully if there is
significant uncertainty about future risk experience, and this may be especially true in
health and care insurance. Nevertheless, some markets demand premium and benefit
guarantees and the actuary must produce appropriate prices.
On the other hand, private insurers always have the option of withdrawing from a particular
market (ie line of business) if it is felt to be too risky.
(i)         Exercise of options by healthy policyholders only
The option would only be worthless if there were only two types of individual – those in perfectly
good health (normal select policyholders), and those who were claiming benefits. There is a third
(and significant) category: those who currently have an increased risk of claiming in the future,
but who are not yet ill (enough) to qualify for a claim at the present time.
An example of someone who would benefit from exercising an option would be an applicant who
is in good health at policy entry, who takes out CI insurance with an option to increase benefits
after ten years, without further medical evidence. By ten years’ time the person may have
developed hypertension (high blood pressure), with much increased risk of stroke, but he or she
can still take out the increased cover on standard terms under the option.
(ii)        How restricting when the option can be exercised reduces anti‐selection
With restrictions on the times at which the option can be exercised, policyholders cannot
immediately exercise the option on discovering that they are a poor risk, eg when they have just
discovered that they have extremely high blood pressure.
(i)       Exercise of options by worse‐than‐select policyholders only
The first costs more to the insurance company – if everyone with higher than select morbidity
takes up the option.
This means that the maximum possible total amount of gain has been made by the policyholders.
This will include the cost to the insurance company of insuring the few very high risk individuals at
standard premiums, plus the costs of insuring the other medium‐high risks on the same terms.
(ii)      Exercise of options by all policyholders
The insurance company is actually better off if all the healthy policyholders take up the option as
well as the unhealthy ones. This is because the healthy policyholders will be paying a premium
that should be appropriate to their expected select morbidity, and (provided the insurance
company’s standard premium rates are profitable) this should create additional profits for the
insurance company.
The existence of the morbidity option increases the morbidity risk. For example, an option to
extend the term increases the time for which the insurer is exposed to risk. Similarly, an option to
increase the sum insured increases the amount that is exposed to risk. The reserves need to be
increased for the expected cost of the increased claims.
However, reserves will also need to increase to reflect the uncertainty in this expected cost.
Alternatively, additional solvency capital will be required to cover these risks.
Before the option is exercised, the insurer is exposed to the risk that policyholders will selectively
choose to exercise the option if their health is worse than for a select life.
Even after the option has been exercised, the insurer is still exposed to considerable uncertainty.
As there is no further underwriting, it has no way of knowing the health of the lives that exercised
the option. Reserves will need to be calculated on the assumption that a level of anti‐selection
has taken place.
It is very difficult to include this degree of uncertainty in a stochastic model with any confidence
about the probability distributions that would need to be assumed. Instead the stochastic model
would probably be run several times incorporating major ‘what‐if’ shifts to the underlying
parameters of the assumed probability distributions.
So, for example, our assumption shifts could imply significant changes to the expected
proportions in each risk group, and to the expected morbidity of each group, and the model
would be rerun to see the effect this might have on the simulated distribution of option costs and
hence on the required option premium.
This is an example of sensitivity testing in a stochastic modelling context.
Syllabus objective: 4.2        Understand and apply the techniques used in pricing health and care insurance
0.1 Mortality and morbidity options: Mortality and morbidity options are parts of a product’s design whereby the policyholder can
choose to extend the term or increase the level of cover at normal premium rates but without
providing further medical evidence. To the extent that the option might be exercised by someone
in poor health, the insurance company will bear a cost: the difference between the ordinary
premium rate granted under the terms of the option and that which would have been granted
had the policyholder been underwritten at the time the option was exercised.
The pricing of options in healthcare insurance is complex and only an indication of the
problems and solutions will be given here. Options have little impact on a one-year contract
(PMI), if the terms and acceptances are variable every year, so the discussion below will
0.2 Guarantees: Conventional without-profits insurance contracts transfer the mortality / morbidity, expense
and investment risk from the policyholder to the insurance company.
Therefore guarantees may relate to premiums, benefits or investments. Premium and benefit
Other contracts leave the investment risk with the policyholder, eg unit-linked endowment
While there may be some investment guarantees provided by insurers under health and
care insurance policies, these are not the norm and thus outside the scope of this subject.
The student is advised to look at the particular sections within Subject SP2 if wanting to
Question: (i)       Why do most health and care insurance contracts not pose financially onerous investment
guarantees on insurers?
(ii)      Suggest a health and care insurance contract that could pose the insurer with a significant
investment risk.
(i)       If people can’t exercise health insurance options when they are sick, why doesn’t this
defeat the object of having the option, and make it worthless to the policyholder?
(ii)      Why does restricting the points of time at which the option can be exercised reduce the
risk of anti‐selection?
(i)       Does the option cost more to the insurance company if every single person with worse‐
than‐select morbidity takes up the option, or if just the small number of very high risk
policyholders do so?
(ii)      Does the option cost more to the insurance company if every single eligible policyholder
takes up the option, or if only those that have higher than select morbidity do so?
Suggest possible courses of action that an insurance company could follow if it found itself having
to honour particularly onerous health options in the way suggested above.
Explain why a policy that contains a morbidity option is likely to need much higher reserves than
the equivalent policy without an option.
Suggest briefly how we could model the effects of the kinds of large changes to option costs
described in Section 0.1, eg as a result of a significant increase in the prevalence of a particular
disease.
It is common for long-term insurance pol:          purchase additional assurance without providing further evidence of health at the
normal premium rates (for a life of that particular age) at the date on which the
         renew a long-term insurance policy, eg a critical illness insurance at the end of its
original term without providing additional evidence of health
         reinstate mortality cover after an accelerated critical illness plan has paid out on a
These are examples of mortality / morbidity options, which may also be referred to simply as
The terms and conditions under which the option can be exercised should be clearly set out
in the original policy. Sometimes an option can only be exercised at fixed points of time,
eg at the end of every five years of a twenty-year term insurance, or at any time providing a
qualifying event has occurred, eg the birth of a child, taking up a new job at a higher salary.
The extent of the option will also be specified, eg the additional sum insured cannot exceed
The terms and conditions for the exercise of the option are designed to reduce the risk of
selection against the insurer, ie an excess of lives in poor health using the options to obtain
large amounts of long-term insurance at premium rates that are insufficient to cover the
There are differences in the kinds of terms and conditions for exercising a health insurance option
compared with those of a pure life insurance option. For example, an option to take out further
life insurance (death benefits) can be exercised provided the policyholder is alive at the option
date, regardless of the state of health. However, for an option providing further IP cover, the
insurer must not allow someone who is currently receiving benefits (or within a deferred or
linked‐claim period) to exercise an option – ie someone already having a condition that would
trigger a claim payment. Nevertheless, someone who recovers might then be eligible to exercise
future options – depending on the terms and conditions of the particular case.
1.2 The cost of options: The cost of an option is the value of the excess of the premium that should, in the light of
full underwriting information, have been charged for the additional insurance over the
normal premium rate that is charged. For some lives the option will have no cost.
People who satisfy the underwriting criteria (or would do, if they were subjected to them) are
referred to as select policyholders (as described in Subject CM1).
If a life who is in good health and who would be expected to satisfy normal underwriting
requirements exercises the option, the option will generate little additional cost. The
exercise of the option by lives in poor health will generate considerable additional costs.
So the exercise of an option by a currently ‘select’ individual incurs the insurer in little extra cost.
The total expected additional costs of an option depend on the health status of those that
choose to exercise the option, and the proportion of lives that choose to exercise the
In general, the smaller the proportion that exercises the option, the worse will be the
subsequent mortality / morbidity experience of those exercising the option. If a substantial
proportion exercises the option, then their subsequent mortality / morbidity experience will
proportion of lives exercising option: average extent that health of lives exercising option is worse than normal
1.3 Constant monitoring: Whatever the current experience, the value of options must be kept under constant review.
It would not be unthinkable for a health insurance option to come ‘into the money’, much as
annuity options did on some deferred pension policies (despite being relatively worthless at
the time of sale). For example, the widespread onset of a particular disease might make any
option to increase coverage without underwriting particularly costly to the insurer.
The comparison here is being made with an annuity option, which is an example of a financial
option, in which the option cost under all policies would turn out to be zero (if interest rates
exceeded a particular guaranteed level) or significantly positive (if interest rates were lower than
Health options aren’t generally as clear cut as this. Statistically, some proportion of policies would
be expected to be ‘in the money’ at the option date because of their worsened health. However,
it seems very unlikely that this proportion could actually reach 100%. Nevertheless, it is possible
that some dramatic change to future conditions – such as a much increased prevalence of a
particular illness – may give value to the option for many more people than originally anticipated,
thereby far exceeding the expected total cost for which the option was originally priced.
 remove the options from new bu:          increase the current standard rates of premium so that the policies being effected under
the option recoup some of the extra option cost, and/or make the option less attractive
and so reduce the amount of take up
         in the extreme, the insurance company could stop issuing the type of policies that are
being taken out under the option, though this could seriously upset the policyholders
 reinsure some of the increased:          reduce the encouragement given to policyholders to exercise the option, eg stop
reminding policyholders about it when they reach the exercise date
2.1 Method: Mortality / morbidity options are normally valued using cashflow projections.
These cashflows would include the additional benefits expected to be payable under the
option and the additional premiums expected to be received in relation to these benefits, to
the extent to which the option is assumed to be taken up. The additional premiums would
be based on the expected premium rates that would be charged to standard lives for the
additional benefit, as at the option exercise date.
So, when policyholders exercise the option, they will pay exactly the same premium as a new
policyholder that has just passed underwriting on that day, ie they will be charged a premium
based on the select tables in use at that time.
The projections should also allow for any additional expenses incurred in the administration
For example, expenses would be incurred if the insurer writes to each policyholder to remind
them of the option before the exercise date. Expenses will then be incurred in processing the
policyholder’s request to exercise the option.
If the purpose of the valuation is pricing the option, then allowance should also be made for
the additional reserves that should be held, both before and after exercise.
Valuing a mortality option therefore req:          the probability that the option will be exercised, at each possible exercise date
         the additional benefit level that will be chosen, if this is at the discretion of the
         the expected mortality / morbidity of the lives who choose to exercise the option
         the expected mortality / morbidity of the lives who choose not to exercise the option
Option take‐up rates: The model may assume that all eligible policyholders will take up the option, and that the
maximum additional benefit will always be taken.
You may see this approach (ie using the assumption that everyone exercises the option) described
as the ‘conventional method’ in past exam questions.
The assumption that everyone will exercise the option is unlikely to be borne out in practice, but
the method does have the advantage of simplicity.
If there are many possible dates on which an option may be exercised, or there is a choice
from several alternative options, the model may assume that the worst option from the
financial point of view of the company is chosen with probability one.
Alternatively, the model may use more sophisticated take-up rate assumptions which vary
by exercise date or by alternative option. These would ideally be based on past experience.
You may see this approach (ie making an assumption about the proportion of policyholders that
exercise the option) described as the ‘North American method’ in past exam questions.
This method uses a more realistic assumption for the option take‐up rate than the first approach
suggested above. However, it may be difficult to obtain the data to make suitable assumptions
(historically this data was only available in North America, hence the name).
Mortality / morbidity rates: Typically, due to anti-selection, the expected mortality / morbidity of lives who take up the
option will be heavier than that of those who do not.
For example, the mortality of those who exercise the option may be assumed to be a higher
percentage of the base mortality table, eg those that exercise the option might be assumed to
experience mortality of 150% of the base mortality table.
Alternatively, an age loading may be applied (eg a policyholder of age x may be assumed to
experience mortality of age x + 5 years).
It may instead be assumed that the mortality experience of those who take up the option will
be the Ultimate experience which corresponds to the Select experience that would have
been used as a basis if underwriting had been completed as normal when the option was
exercised. This would be consistent with an assumption that all eligible policyholders take
up the option.
So, this would be the approach to use under the ‘conventional method’ described above.
As noted earlier, there should be a link between the assumed option take-up rates and the
assumed mortality rates.
It may be assumed that the lives who do not take up the option will continue to experience
the same level of mortality as would have been assumed without the existence of the
option. However, this would mean that the average mortality for all lives has been assumed
to be in excess of the base mortality assumption, since those taking up the option are
assumed to experience higher mortality than this level.
For example, if lives that take up the option are assumed to be ‘ultimate plus ten years’ and the
lives that do not take up the option are assumed to be ‘ultimate’, then the average of their
mortality must be heavier than ultimate.
However, the average mortality of all lives (both those that take and do not take the option)
would be expected to be ultimate. To address this, the following alternative approach could be
used.
An alternative assumption would therefore be that the mortality of those who do not take up
the option is such that average mortality for all lives remains at the base expected level.
The assumed mortality of those who do not take up the option would then be lower than this
base level.
For example, under this alternative approach, the lives that take up the option could be assumed
to be ‘ultimate plus ten years’ and the average mortality would be assumed to be ultimate. If the
proportion that take up the option is 25% (say), then a mortality assumption could be determined

Whilst the example outlined above discuss a mortality example, similar comments would
apply for morbidity.
2.3 Stochastic modelling: The core cost of options can be established through stochastic modelling. The future
experience is projected and the numbers taking up the various options and their
subsequent claim propensities are investigated. A large number of simulations will be
tested and the cost of the option will be calculated with a particular statistical degree of
The model would have to subdivide the population of policyholders into different risk categories,
such as groups (1) and (2) as defined in the previous section, and quite possibly including
additional subgroups within these. The proportions ending up in each risk group and also the
average claim experience of each risk group at the option date could be modelled as stochastic
variables. We would therefore need appropriate probability distributions for these.
3.1 Resilience testing of premium rate: Premium rates need to be assessed on a resilience basis to judge whether, in the absence
of an ability to change the premium / benefit relationship, the premium charged will be
sufficient to meet all outgoings with an agreed level of confidence.
This is essentially the process of sensitivity testing, that was covered in Chapter 11.
This will require the establishment of reserves in early years when the risk is lower and
That is, the claims experience early in the policy term should have been fairly accurately
anticipated (ie have low volatility or low risk). However, the insurance company needs to reserve
adequately at all times in order to cover potential adverse volatility from future claims, which will
3.2 Adequacy of reserves in pricing: The actuary needs to incorporate into the premium a loading to cover the additional cost of
providing the guarantee (eg as determined under such a resilience test), including the cost
If the true cost of guarantees appears in the premium, it may encourage many to move to a
reviewable contract, where the same benefits (but without the guarantee) will cost
Incorporating the cost of reserves and reviewability were discussed in Chapter 19.
3.3 Regulatory approval: The local regulator will normally want to see evidence of the techniques employed and their
output, to judge the adequacy of the premiums and reserves required.
Mortality / morbidity (health) options: Many health and care insurance contracts contain options whereby the policyholder can
choose to extend the term or increase the level of cover at normal premium rates, ie without
To the extent that the option might be exercised by someone in poor health, the insurer will
bear a cost: the difference between the ordinary premium rate granted under the terms of
the option and that which would have been granted had the individual been underwritten.
Mortality / morbidity options can be valued using cashflow projections. Assumptions are

Experience must be monitored in order to identify potential large risk threats that could
Cost of guarantees: Claim and expense guarantees under health and care insurance contracts can be onerous.
Investment guarantees are generally less of an issue except with LTCI.
Guarantees require larger risk margins to be included in product prices in order to cover
Sensitivity testing of guaranteed premium rates should be performed under a range of
changed conditions, to ensure margins are large enough.
Reserves may need to be significantly increased for guaranteed products. The full cost of
these reserves must be included in the pricing model, increasing the cost of capital and so
further increasing the price of the product.
